data.title: Magna Mining (TSXV:NICU) Trailing 12-Month Net Income Turns Positive, Challenging Bearish Narratives
data.description:
Magna Mining (TSXV:NICU) has reported its Q3 2025 financial results, posting total revenue of CA$16.3 million and basic EPS of -CA$0.05 for the period, with net income (excluding extra items) at -CA$10.6 million. The company’s revenue over recent quarters has shifted from CA$4.5 million in Q1 to CA$18.5 million in Q2, before landing at CA$16.3 million in Q3. EPS moved from CA$0.15 in Q1 to negative territory in the two following quarters. As profit margins have fluctuated throughout the year, investors will be weighing these numbers with an eye on sustainability, especially as attention turns to future growth drivers and long-term trends.

See our full analysis for Magna Mining.

Next up, we benchmark these headline results against the latest market narratives to see which stories stand up to scrutiny and which might be in for a rethink.

Profitability Flip: Trailing 12-Month Net Income Turns Positive

  • Magna Mining reported net income of $4.24 million over the last twelve months, a marked shift from quarterly losses such as -$10.64 million this quarter and large negatives seen in prior periods.
  • This transition to positive net income stands out for a company that, just several quarters ago, reported no revenue and persistent losses. This underscores a material pivot in operating results.
    • The net income improvement backs up forecasts for annual earnings growth of 88.05%, showing that the company’s profitability is not simply a one-off event but part of a broader momentum.
    • Still, critics highlight that recent quarterly net income is deeply negative, suggesting the path to sustainable profits may remain uneven. Trailing numbers reflect cumulative gains that could soften if single quarters disappoint.
  • Consensus narrative notes the shift from unprofitable to profitable over the past year is a clear positive, but ongoing volatility, visible in quarterly swings, remains a watch item for long-term investors.
    📊 Read the full Magna Mining Consensus Narrative.

Premium Price Tag: Valuation Signals Investor Optimism

  • Magna Mining’s Price-To-Sales Ratio is 17.1x, which is triple the Canadian sector average of 5.6x but lower than the peer average of 22.3x. The current share price of CA$2.68 sits more than four times above the DCF fair value of CA$0.63.
  • The relevance of premium pricing to the bullish narrative is nuanced. Investors are betting on outsized future growth, but this optimism is already reflected in valuation, leaving limited margin for error if growth slows.
    • Bulls argue strong revenue momentum and a return to profitability justify the premium, especially with forecasts calling for annual revenue growth of 61.9%, which is well above the broader Canadian market’s 11.7%.
    • However, numbers like the DCF fair value (CA$0.63 versus current price CA$2.68) underscore that investors are clearly pricing in aggressive long-term success, so any disappointment could weigh on sentiment.

Non-Cash Earnings and Dilution: Quality of Profits Under Microscope

  • Over the past year, reported profits included a high proportion of non-cash earnings and there was shareholder dilution, making the quality of recent profitability a key point of discussion.
  • While narrative momentum has turned with the first period of sustained profitability, investors are urged to keep an eye on how much of those profits come from true operations as opposed to accounting adjustments or financing activities.
    • The presence of dilution introduces a long-term risk, as it can erode per-share value even if total profits are growing.
    • High non-cash earnings mean that bulls and bears alike need to scrutinize cash generation and not just headline profit numbers when assessing real business strength.

data.title: Is Altus Group Fairly Priced After Property Tech Expansion and Executive Hire?

Approach 1: Altus Group Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) valuation model estimates a company's worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those totals back to today's value. This approach helps investors gauge what the business might be worth if those future cash flows materialize as expected.

For Altus Group, the current Free Cash Flow stands at CA$65.2 million. Approach 2: Altus Group Price vs Earnings

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is widely used to value companies that are consistently profitable. It tells investors how much they are paying for each dollar of earnings, making it a popular benchmark for gauging whether a stock is expensive or cheap relative to its profit-generating ability.

A company's fair PE ratio can vary depending on its future growth prospects and the risks associated with its business. Higher expected earnings growth or lower risk typically justify a higher PE ratio, while slower growth or more uncertainty can put downward pressure on a fair multiple. Comparing a company's PE ratio to those of similar businesses and the broader industry can offer some context, but these metrics alone miss some important nuances.

Currently, Altus Group trades at a PE ratio of 89.16x. That is considerably higher than both the real estate industry average of 14.76x and the average of select peers at 36.11x. Because the Fair Ratio is tailored to the company and incorporates a broader set of fundamentals than a simple comparison with peers or industry averages, it offers a more accurate indication of whether the current PE is justified. Altus Group’s actual PE is significantly higher than its Fair Ratio, suggesting that, relative to its fundamentals and market environment, the stock is overvalued based on this approach.

data.title: Cheetah Mobile (NYSE:CMCM) Q3 Net Loss Narrows, Challenging Pessimistic Community Narratives
data.description:
Cheetah Mobile (CMCM) just reported its Q3 2025 financial results, posting revenue of 287.4 million CNY and an EPS of -0.35 CNY. Looking back, the company has seen revenue climb from 192.1 million CNY in Q3 2024 to 287.4 million CNY in the latest quarter; however, EPS has remained negative throughout. While margins are still compressed, this performance sets the table for what investors expect in the coming quarters.

Losses Narrow, but Remain Large

  • Net income excluding extraordinary items improved from -22.6 million CNY last quarter to -11.0 million CNY this quarter, continuing a trend of shrinking losses from -33.4 million CNY in Q1 2025 and -46.9 million CNY in Q3 2024. The figure remains negative, but the losses have become less severe each period.
  • The prevailing market view highlights that Cheetah Mobile’s strategic pivot toward AI and robotics is underway. This costly transformation, however, keeps the company in a loss-making mode, and critics emphasize losses are accumulating even as topline momentum emerges.
    • Income statements show consistent net losses every quarter over the past year, underscoring the expense of transitioning into new business lines.
    • Notably, operating costs are being managed more effectively with each quarter. Margins remain negative, but the reduction in net loss size suggests that financial discipline is starting to show.

Revenue Growth Outpaces Industry

  • Trailing twelve-month revenue rose to 1.08 billion CNY, growing at a 21.4% forecasted annual pace. This is double the broader US market’s average rate of 10.5% per year, despite CMCM’s ongoing lack of profitability.
  • Supporters argue that Cheetah Mobile’s ability to drive revenue growth well above the industry average could position it as a standout in the software sector, especially since analysts cite new AI and robotics initiatives as the main growth drivers.

    • The current price-to-sales ratio of 1.5x, which is well below the US software peer average of 4.8x, strengthens the bullish argument for potential upside if growth continues.

  • CMCM’s price-to-sales ratio sits at 1.5x, which is markedly lower than the industry average of 4.8x and its peer group’s 3.8x. This reflects discounted market expectations, even with significant share price volatility over the last year.
  • While the prevailing opinion sees this valuation as a margin of safety, it also highlights an ongoing tension: investors remain cautious as the company works to address persistent net losses, and the lower multiple could remain until meaningful earnings progress is demonstrated.
    • Share price volatility stands out as a notable risk factor, with no other major risks flagged in the past year. This keeps valuation sentiment balanced between growth potential and execution risk.

data.title: Is Now The Time To Put Apollo Hospitals Enterprise (NSE:APOLLOHOSP) On Your Watchlist?
data.description:
The excitement of investing in a company that can reverse its fortunes is a big draw for some speculators, so even companies that have no revenue, no profit, and a record of falling short, can manage to find investors. Unfortunately, these high risk investments often have little probability of ever paying off, and many investors pay a price to learn their lesson. Loss-making companies are always racing against time to reach financial sustainability, so investors in these companies may be taking on more risk than they should.

In contrast to all that, many investors prefer to focus on companies like Apollo Hospitals Enterprise (NSE:APOLLOHOSP), which has not only revenues, but also profits. Now this is not to say that the company presents the best investment opportunity around, but profitability is a key component to success in business.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

How Fast Is Apollo Hospitals Enterprise Growing?

If you believe that markets are even vaguely efficient, then over the long term you'd expect a company's share price to follow its earnings per share (EPS) outcomes. That means EPS growth is considered a real positive by most successful long-term investors. It certainly is nice to see that Apollo Hospitals Enterprise has managed to grow EPS by 26% per year over three years. As a result, we can understand why the stock trades on a high multiple of trailing twelve month earnings.

It's often helpful to take a look at earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) margins, as well as revenue growth, to get another take on the quality of the company's growth. While we note Apollo Hospitals Enterprise achieved similar EBIT margins to last year, revenue grew by a solid 14% to ₹233b. That's progress.

In the chart below, you can see how the company has grown earnings and revenue, over time. Click on the chart to see the exact numbers.

Check out our latest analysis for Apollo Hospitals Enterprise

While we live in the present moment, there's little doubt that the future matters most in the investment decision process. So why not check this interactive chart depicting future EPS estimates, for Apollo Hospitals Enterprise?

Are Apollo Hospitals Enterprise Insiders Aligned With All Shareholders?

Owing to the size of Apollo Hospitals Enterprise, we wouldn't expect insiders to hold a significant proportion of the company. But thanks to their investment in the company, it's pleasing to see that there are still incentives to align their actions with the shareholders. Notably, they have an enviable stake in the company, worth ₹96b. This suggests that leadership will be very mindful of shareholders' interests when making decisions!

While it's always good to see some strong conviction in the company from insiders through heavy investment, it's also important for shareholders to ask if management compensation policies are reasonable. Our quick analysis into CEO remuneration would seem to indicate they are. Our analysis has discovered that the median total compensation for the CEOs of companies like Apollo Hospitals Enterprise, with market caps over ₹714b, is about ₹103m.

Apollo Hospitals Enterprise offered total compensation worth ₹82m to its CEO in the year to March 2025. That comes in below the average for similar sized companies and seems pretty reasonable. CEO compensation is hardly the most important aspect of a company to consider, but when it's reasonable, that gives a little more confidence that leadership are looking out for shareholder interests. It can also be a sign of good governance, more generally.

Should You Add Apollo Hospitals Enterprise To Your Watchlist?

For growth investors, Apollo Hospitals Enterprise's raw rate of earnings growth is a beacon in the night. If you still have your doubts, remember too that company insiders have a considerable investment aligning themselves with the shareholders and CEO pay is quite modest compared to similarly sized companiess. This may only be a fast rundown, but the key takeaway is that Apollo Hospitals Enterprise is worth keeping an eye on. Of course, profit growth is one thing but it's even better if Apollo Hospitals Enterprise is receiving high returns on equity, since that should imply it can keep growing without much need for capital. Although Apollo Hospitals Enterprise certainly looks good, it may appeal to more investors if insiders were buying up shares. data.title: Is Stellantis a Hidden Opportunity After 29% Drop and Electric Vehicle Investments?
data.description:
• Wondering if Stellantis is a hidden gem or just another player in the auto sector? Let's take a closer look at whether its current stock price might offer genuine value for investors like you.
  • After a brief 5.9% jump last week, Stellantis shares have still fallen 29.1% year-to-date, highlighting both the potential for a rebound and fresh concerns about risk from the market.
  • Recently, Stellantis made headlines with bold investments in electric vehicle technology and new joint ventures. These moves have fueled renewed optimism and some controversy about its future competitiveness. Regulatory conversations across Europe, especially around emissions and tariffs, are also creating both new challenges and buzz for the company.
  • On our proprietary scale, Stellantis earns a 5 out of 6 valuation score, which suggests it is undervalued across most key measures. Next, we'll break down how we arrive at this score using familiar valuation approaches, but stick around as the best way to judge Stellantis’ value might surprise you at the end.

Find out why Stellantis's -19.7% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Stellantis Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates a company's intrinsic value by projecting its future free cash flows and then discounting them back to their present value. This approach aims to determine what Stellantis is fundamentally worth based on its ability to generate cash in euros over time.

Currently, Stellantis reports a trailing twelve months Free Cash Flow (FCF) of approximately -€10.91 billion, reflecting recent investment cycles and market challenges. Analysts provide forecasted FCFs for the next five years, with projections showing a significant turnaround. By 2029, Stellantis’ FCF is anticipated to reach about €5.45 billion. Beyond the five-year horizon, Simply Wall St extrapolates further growth and expects FCF to exceed €6.75 billion by 2034.

Based on these cash flow projections and using the 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model, the DCF method calculates Stellantis’ fair value at €20.03 per share. Compared to its current market price, this figure signals a substantial 55.3% discount, suggesting the stock is trading well below what its fundamental cash flows imply it should be worth.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Stellantis is undervalued by 55.3%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 927 more undervalued stocks based on cash flows.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Stellantis.

Approach 2: Stellantis Price vs Sales

The Price-to-Sales (P/S) ratio is often the preferred valuation metric for companies like Stellantis, particularly when profits are volatile or temporarily negative but revenue remains robust. For auto manufacturers, where margins can fluctuate due to cyclical demand and heavy investments, the P/S ratio lets investors gauge the market's valuation of every euro of sales the company generates, regardless of recent earnings swings.

While the P/S ratio itself does not account for profit margins or growth directly, it is heavily influenced by both. A company expected to grow sales quickly or with a strong competitive position typically commands a higher P/S. Conversely, increased risk, slowing sales, or economic headwinds can push the P/S ratio lower. What is considered 'fair' will also reflect the industry context, where the average Auto sector company currently trades on a P/S of 0.79x, and Stellantis' peer group averages 2.55x.

Stellantis is currently trading at a P/S ratio of just 0.18x, which is far below both the industry average and its peers. However, rather than relying solely on these benchmarks, Simply Wall St uses a "Fair Ratio" of 0.46x, which takes into account Stellantis’ growth outlook, profit potential, risk profile, and market capitalization to offer a more tailored comparison. This proprietary metric provides a more holistic valuation assessment than simply comparing company to company or with the broad industry, since it reflects the specifics that matter to each individual business and investor.

Putting it all together, with Stellantis' actual P/S ratio at 0.18x and the Fair Ratio at 0.46x, the stock appears significantly undervalued based on this method.

Result: UNDERVALUED

A Narrative is a clear, user-defined story explaining why you think a company will succeed or struggle. It ties together your personal perspective with forecasts for the company's revenue, earnings, and margins to arrive at a fair value.

data.title: Autodesk (ADSK) Margin Decline to 16.1% Raises Questions on Bullish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Autodesk (ADSK) just reported its Q3 2026 results, posting revenue of $1.85 billion and EPS of $1.61 for the quarter. Over the past three quarters, revenue has moved up from $1.63 billion to $1.76 billion and then to $1.85 billion, while EPS climbed from $0.71 to $1.47 and now to $1.61. Margins did compress over the last year, so investors will be closely watching how that trend plays out from here.

See our full analysis for Autodesk.

Now that the headline numbers are in, we’ll put Autodesk’s latest performance up against the prevailing narratives to see which stories hold up and which might be overdue for a re-think.

See what the community is saying about Autodesk

Advertisement

Margins Slide to 16.1%

  • Net profit margins dropped from 18.3% to 16.1% over the last year, tracking a persistent margin compression even as revenue grew to $1.85 billion in Q3 2026.
  • Analysts' consensus view highlights concern over this decline, noting that margin pressure could mute some of the benefits from recurring revenue and SaaS adoption.
    • Even though subscription and SaaS models are designed to stabilize and potentially expand margins, the margin outcome over the trailing twelve months emphasizes the need for efficiency improvements.
    • This drop in profitability is notable given the company’s ongoing innovation investments, which are expected to strengthen future net margin trends but may have contributed to shorter-term margin contraction.

Many see Autodesk’s margin trajectory as a key test for consensus optimism. The central question remains: will recurring revenue gains be enough to reverse this slippage, or do investors need to brace for further pressure? 📊 Read the full Autodesk Consensus Narrative.

Premium Valuation Versus Peers

  • Autodesk trades at a Price-To-Earnings ratio of 57.7x, which is lower than its peer average of 66x but still significantly higher than the US software industry average of 29.2x, and above its DCF fair value of $282.27 versus today’s share price of $301.38.
  • The consensus narrative suggests investors still expect outsized earnings growth (18.65% annually) to justify this premium, especially with analysts forecasting a 9.4% upside to a $364.52 price target.
    • This premium is underpinned by belief in the durability of Autodesk’s high-quality earnings and its strategic positioning in cloud and AI, but it leaves less margin for error if competition or margin slippage accelerates.
    • Notably, the current price already bakes in much of the bullish outlook. This means execution risk, especially on margins and topline growth, remains material for equity holders.

Insider Selling Adds a Note of Caution

  • Significant insider selling took place in the past three months. This is a potential yellow flag even as broader risk indicators have remained relatively stable in the latest filings.
  • Consensus narrative makes the point that, while insider sales can reflect many factors, persistent selling amid margin contraction and premium valuation invites a more cautious read on near-term upside.
    • While analysts remain confident in double-digit revenue and earnings growth for the next few years, insider disposition tempers the overall bullish momentum.
    • This development serves as a reminder that strong analyst projections and innovative business models still need to deliver real, sustained profitability to keep shares on their upward path.

Approach 1: Berkshire Hathaway Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns Model is designed to estimate a company’s intrinsic value by focusing on the profits generated above its cost of capital. This approach calculates how efficiently Berkshire Hathaway reinvests its profits to generate additional returns for shareholders over the long run.

For Berkshire Hathaway, this model uses a Book Value of $485,274.36 per share and a Stable Earnings Per Share (EPS) of $66,154.88, which is based on the company’s median return on equity over the last five years. Shareholders will be happy to know that Protector Forsikring's EPS has grown 28% each year, compound, over three years. If the company can sustain that sort of growth, we'd expect shareholders to come away satisfied.

It's often helpful to take a look at earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) margins, as well as revenue growth, to get another take on the quality of the company's growth. data.title: Fluidra, S.A. (BME:FDR) Will Pay A €0.243 Dividend In Three Days
data.description:
Some investors rely on dividends for growing their wealth, and if you're one of those dividend sleuths, you might be intrigued to know that Fluidra, S.A. (BME:FDR) is about to go ex-dividend in just three days. The ex-dividend date generally occurs two days before the record date, which is the day on which shareholders need to be on the company's books in order to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is of consequence because whenever a stock is bought or sold, the trade can take two business days or more to settle. Thus, you can purchase Fluidra's shares before the 1st of December in order to receive the dividend, which the company will pay on the 3rd of December.

The company's next dividend payment will be €0.243 per share, and in the last 12 months, the company paid a total of €0.60 per share. Based on the last year's worth of payments, Fluidra stock has a trailing yield of around 2.5% on the current share price of €24.14. If you buy this business for its dividend, you should have an idea of whether Fluidra's dividend is reliable and sustainable. We need to see whether the dividend is covered by earnings and if it's growing.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Dividends are typically paid out of company income, so if a company pays out more than it earned, its dividend is usually at a higher risk of being cut. Fluidra paid out 70% of its earnings to investors last year, a normal payout level for most businesses. A useful secondary check can be to evaluate whether Fluidra generated enough free cash flow to afford its dividend. Over the last year it paid out 59% of its free cash flow as dividends, within the usual range for most companies.

It's encouraging to see that the dividend is covered by both profit and cash flow. This generally suggests the dividend is sustainable, as long as earnings don't drop precipitously.

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Businesses with strong growth prospects usually make the best dividend payers, because it's easier to grow dividends when earnings per share are improving. If business enters a downturn and the dividend is cut, the company could see its value fall precipitously. That's why it's comforting to see Fluidra's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 85% per annum for the past five years. Management appears to be striking a nice balance between reinvesting for growth and paying dividends to shareholders. Earnings per share have been growing quickly and in combination with some reinvestment and a middling payout ratio, the stock may have decent dividend prospects going forwards.

The main way most investors will assess a company's dividend prospects is by checking the historical rate of dividend growth. In the last 10 years, Fluidra has lifted its dividend by approximately 26% a year on average. Both per-share earnings and dividends have both been growing rapidly in recent times, which is great to see.

Final Takeaway

Should investors buy Fluidra for the upcoming dividend? It's good to see earnings are growing, since all of the best dividend stocks grow their earnings meaningfully over the long run. However, we'd also note that Fluidra is paying out more than half of its earnings and cash flow as profits, which could limit the dividend growth if earnings growth slows. data.title: The Return Trends At TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale (BIT:TFIN) Look Promising
data.description:
What trends should we look for it we want to identify stocks that can multiply in value over the long term? Ideally, a business will show two trends; firstly a growing return on capital employed (ROCE) and secondly, an increasing amount of capital employed. If you see this, it typically means it's a company with a great business model and plenty of profitable reinvestment opportunities. Speaking of which, we noticed some great changes in TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale's (BIT:TFIN) returns on capital, so let's have a look.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

Return On Capital Employed (ROCE): What Is It?

For those that aren't sure what ROCE is, it measures the amount of pre-tax profits a company can generate from the capital employed in its business. The formula for this calculation on TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale is:

Return on Capital Employed = Earnings Before Interest and Tax (EBIT) ÷ (Total Assets - Current Liabilities)

0.15 = €60m ÷ (€700m - €304m) (Based on the trailing twelve months to June 2025).

So, TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale has an ROCE of 15%. In isolation, that's a pretty standard return but against the Construction industry average of 23%, it's not as good.

See our latest analysis for TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale

In the above chart we have measured TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale's prior ROCE against its prior performance, but the future is arguably more important. If you'd like, you can check out the forecasts from the analysts covering TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale for free.

What Does the ROCE Trend For TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale Tell Us?

We're delighted to see that TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale is reaping rewards from its investments and has now broken into profitability. The company was generating losses five years ago, but has managed to turn it around and as we saw earlier is now earning 15%, which is always encouraging. While returns have increased, the amount of capital employed by TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale has remained flat over the period. With no noticeable increase in capital employed, it's worth knowing what the company plans on doing going forward in regards to reinvesting and growing the business. After all, a company can only become a long term multi-bagger if it continually reinvests in itself at high rates of return.

Another thing to note, TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale has a high ratio of current liabilities to total assets of 43%. This effectively means that suppliers (or short-term creditors) are funding a large portion of the business, so just be aware that this can introduce some elements of risk. Ideally we'd like to see this reduce as that would mean fewer obligations bearing risks.

What We Can Learn From TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale's ROCE

As discussed above, TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale appears to be getting more proficient at generating returns since capital employed has remained flat but earnings (before interest and tax) are up. Given the stock has declined 59% in the last five years, this could be a good investment if the valuation and other metrics are also appealing. So researching this company further and determining whether or not these trends will continue seems justified.

TREVI - Finanziaria Industriale does come with some risks though, we found 2 warning signs in our investment analysis, and 1 of those is potentially serious...

data.title: Mishorim Real Estate Investments (TLV:MSHR) Seems To Be Using A Lot Of Debt
data.description:
David Iben put it well when he said, 'Volatility is not a risk we care about. What we care about is avoiding the permanent loss of capital.' So it might be obvious that you need to consider debt, when you think about how risky any given stock is, because too much debt can sink a company. As with many other companies Mishorim Real Estate Investments Ltd (TLV:MSHR) makes use of debt. But is this debt a concern to shareholders?

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Advertisement

What Risk Does Debt Bring?

Debt assists a business until the business has trouble paying it off, either with new capital or with free cash flow. Part and parcel of capitalism is the process of 'creative destruction' where failed businesses are mercilessly liquidated by their bankers. However, a more frequent (but still costly) occurrence is where a company must issue shares at bargain-basement prices, permanently diluting shareholders, just to shore up its balance sheet. By replacing dilution, though, debt can be an extremely good tool for businesses that need capital to invest in growth at high rates of return. The first step when considering a company's debt levels is to consider its cash and debt together.

How Much Debt Does Mishorim Real Estate Investments Carry?

The chart below, which you can click on for greater detail, shows that Mishorim Real Estate Investments had ₪1.53b in debt in September 2025; about the same as the year before. On the flip side, it has ₪330.9m in cash leading to net debt of about ₪1.20b.

A Look At Mishorim Real Estate Investments' Liabilities

Zooming in on the latest balance sheet data, we can see that Mishorim Real Estate Investments had liabilities of ₪390.4m due within 12 months and liabilities of ₪1.45b due beyond that. On the other hand, it had cash of ₪330.9m and ₪44.8m worth of receivables due within a year. So its liabilities outweigh the sum of its cash and (near-term) receivables by ₪1.47b.

The deficiency here weighs heavily on the ₪185.4m company itself, as if a child were struggling under the weight of an enormous back-pack full of books, his sports gear, and a trumpet. So we definitely think shareholders need to watch this one closely. After all, Mishorim Real Estate Investments would likely require a major re-capitalisation if it had to pay its creditors today.

See our latest analysis for Mishorim Real Estate Investments

In order to size up a company's debt relative to its earnings, we calculate its net debt divided by its earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA) and its earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) divided by its interest expense (its interest cover). Thus we consider debt relative to earnings both with and without depreciation and amortization expenses.

Weak interest cover of 0.31 times and a disturbingly high net debt to EBITDA ratio of 11.6 hit our confidence in Mishorim Real Estate Investments like a one-two punch to the gut. This means we'd consider it to have a heavy debt load. The good news is that Mishorim Real Estate Investments grew its EBIT a smooth 73% over the last twelve months. Like a mother's loving embrace of a newborn that sort of growth builds resilience, putting the company in a stronger position to manage its debt. The balance sheet is clearly the area to focus on when you are analysing debt. But it is Mishorim Real Estate Investments's earnings that will influence how the balance sheet holds up in the future. So when considering debt, it's definitely worth looking at the earnings trend. Click here for an interactive snapshot.

Finally, a company can only pay off debt with cold hard cash, not accounting profits. So we always check how much of that EBIT is translated into free cash flow. During the last three years, Mishorim Real Estate Investments burned a lot of cash. While that may be a result of expenditure for growth, it does make the debt far more risky.

Our View

To be frank both Mishorim Real Estate Investments's conversion of EBIT to free cash flow and its track record of staying on top of its total liabilities make us rather uncomfortable with its debt levels. But at least it's pretty decent at growing its EBIT; that's encouraging. Taking into account all the aforementioned factors, it looks like Mishorim Real Estate Investments has too much debt. That sort of riskiness is ok for some, but it certainly doesn't float our boat. When analysing debt levels, the balance sheet is the obvious place to start. However, not all investment risk resides within the balance sheet - far from it. For example, we've discovered 3 warning signs for Mishorim Real Estate Investments (2 make us uncomfortable!) that you should be aware of before investing here.

If, after all that, you're more interested in a fast growing company with a rock-solid balance sheet, then check out our list of net cash growth stocks without delay.
